meta.scene_summary: We're busy building the future of AI, while the government and the power grid are still trying to figure out how to keep the lights on.

cooking.headline: The US government is now the gatekeeper for your AI API keys
cooking.summary: OpenAI is letting the government decide who gets to use their latest model, effectively turning federal bureaucracy into a literal project manager. It's comforting to know that before you can run an inference, you might need to wait for a security clearance that takes longer than a sprint cycle.
cooking.plain_english: The United States government will now vet and approve which users and organizations are permitted to access OpenAI's most advanced artificial intelligence model.

cooking.headline: Anthropic gets a hall pass to release Mythos to the 'cool kids' club
cooking.summary: Anthropic is rolling out Mythos exclusively to trusted organizations, which sounds exactly like that one teacher who only let the popular kids use the good markers. I guess we'll just be here waiting for the public release while the 'trusted' entities start their exclusive speedrun of the future.
cooking.plain_english: Anthropic has received government authorization to release its Mythos artificial intelligence model to a selected group of verified organizations within the United States.

cooking.headline: Elon is bringing Starlink to your phone, because the dead zones in your apartment weren't annoying enough.
cooking.summary: SpaceX is launching a mobile service that promises to keep you connected even when you're literally off the map. It is the perfect solution for anyone who thinks their boss needs an easier way to reach them during a hiking trip.
cooking.plain_english: SpaceX is expanding its satellite internet network to provide direct mobile phone connectivity across the United States.

cooking.headline: The open-source AI gap is closing faster than my browser tabs
cooking.summary: Open weights models are finally nipping at the heels of the proprietary giants, proving that if you give the internet enough compute, they will eventually replicate your secret sauce. It turns out the 'moat' around your frontier model is more like a sandcastle at high tide.
cooking.plain_english: Open-source artificial intelligence models are becoming increasingly competitive with the most advanced private models.

cooking.headline: AI companies are spending millions on lobbying like they're trying to buy a DLC for democracy
cooking.summary: The AI industry is dumping massive amounts of cash into US elections to ensure their regulation stays light. It's the ultimate group project where the richest member just pays the teacher to grade the whole thing as an A.
cooking.plain_english: Major artificial intelligence companies are contributing significant financial resources to influence the outcome of United States elections.

cooking.headline: Asian startups are speedrunning Anthropic's homework while the export ban is in effect
cooking.summary: As export restrictions block access to top-tier models, Asian startups are just launching their own versions of the tech. It's the classic school move of copying the answers from the kid who isn't allowed to share their notes.
cooking.plain_english: Startups in Asia are developing and releasing their own versions of advanced artificial intelligence models due to ongoing restrictions on exporting American technology.

cooking.headline: Sixty AI agents in a trench coat, now with guardrails
cooking.summary: AgentKits is offering sixty pre-built AI agents, which is perfect for anyone who wants to automate their entire workflow into a state of total chaos. It's like assigning a massive group project where the guardrails are the only thing stopping the students from burning the classroom down.
cooking.plain_english: A new collection of sixty pre-configured artificial intelligence agents with built-in safety features has been released for developers.

cooked.headline: PlayStation reminds us that 'owning' digital content is just a really long-term rental agreement.
cooked.summary: Sony is deleting hundreds of purchased movies from user accounts, proving that your digital library is just a subscription you forgot to cancel. It turns out the 'buy' button is really just a 'lease until we decide otherwise' button.
cooked.plain_english: PlayStation is removing access to hundreds of purchased movies from customer accounts due to licensing agreements with a content provider.

cooked.headline: Local politicians are realizing that data centers are the new NIMBY boss fight
cooked.summary: Data centers are guzzling so much power that residents are starting to treat them like the villain in a small-town drama. It is hard to explain to a voter why their lights are flickering just so a cluster of GPUs can finish a training run.
cooked.plain_english: Communities are increasingly opposing the construction of new data centers because of their high electricity consumption and impact on local power grids.

cooked.headline: When the elite nation-state hackers trip over their own shoelaces
cooked.summary: A sophisticated nation-state attack failed because even professional bad actors are apparently prone to the same amateur logic errors as a junior dev. It turns out that having infinite resources doesn't help when your code is just as fragile as a group project submitted five minutes before the deadline.
cooked.plain_english: A detailed technical breakdown explains how a high-level cyberattack failed due to basic mistakes made by the perpetrators.
